title: The History of Libraries
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Libraries have (0) been centres of learning and knowledge preservation for thousands of years. The earliest known libraries date back to ancient Mesopotamia, where clay tablets containing cuneiform writing were carefully catalogued and stored.||The most famous ancient library was (9) the Library of Alexandria in Egypt, which was established in the 3rd century BCE. It aimed to collect all the world's knowledge and is estimated (10) to have contained hundreds of thousands of scrolls. Sadly, the library was destroyed, and much of its collection (11) was lost forever, representing an irreplaceable loss to human knowledge.||During the Middle Ages, libraries were primarily found (12) in monasteries, where monks painstakingly copied manuscripts by hand. The invention of the printing press in the 15th century revolutionised access to books, making (13) them much more affordable and widely available. Public libraries began to emerge in the 19th century, reflecting (14) a belief that knowledge should be accessible to all, not just the wealthy.||Today, libraries continue to evolve. (15) While they still lend books, many now offer digital resources, community spaces, and technology access. Some people worry that libraries will become obsolete in the digital age, but evidence suggests they remain vital community resources, adapting (16) so as to meet changing needs while preserving their fundamental mission of providing free access to information and learning.

example.text: Libraries have (0) centres of learning and knowledge preservation for thousands of years.

questions.explanation: Definite article referring to a specific and famous library.
Estructura pasiva con infinitivo: 'is estimated to have contained'.
Voz pasiva pasada: 'was lost' con sujeto singular.
Preposition 'in' indicates location in the monasteries.
Concessive conjunction that introduces a contrast with evolution.
Part of the fixed expression 'so as to', used to indicate purpose or intention.
